title: Neural Networks
blurb: Fully-connected layers and backpropagation, the activation-function zoo (Sigmoid, Tanh, ReLU & variants, Softmax), and classification vs regression losses (CE, BCE, MSE, MAE). The core mechanics the exam tests every single time.

knowledge_points.title: Neural Networks & Fully-Connected Layers
knowledge_points.recap: A neural network is a composition of functions $y=h(g(f(x)))$. A **fully-connected (affine / linear) layer** computes $Y = XW + b$ with $X\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times D}$, $W\in\mathbb{R}^{D\times M}$, $b\in\mathbb{R}^{1\times M}$. FC layers see **all** inputs (global features), need a **fixed input size**, and are **linear** — two stacked affine layers collapse into one, so non-linear activations are required between them.

questions.q: Given two affine layers $y_1 = XW_1 + B_1$ and $y_2 = y_1 W_2 + B_2$, show they can be written as a single affine layer. What does this imply?
questions.answer:
$y_2 = (XW_1+B_1)W_2 + B_2 = X(W_1 W_2) + (B_1 W_2 + B_2)$, which is again of the form $XW' + b'$ with $W' = W_1W_2$ and $b' = B_1W_2+B_2$.

**Implication:** stacking linear layers without a non-linearity between them gives no extra expressive power — you need **non-linear activation functions** to build deep, expressive networks.
questions.extend: This is *the* justification for activation functions. Without them a 100-layer net = one linear layer. Composition of linear maps is linear.

questions.q: What is true about fully-connected layers?

options.text: They are non-linear functions.
They can be represented as a convolutional layer.
Given input $X_{N\times D}$ and $W_{D\times M}$, a following batch-norm layer's learnable $\beta,\gamma$ have length $D$.
Once initialized, they can accept any input size.

questions.answer: Only **“can be represented as a convolutional layer”** is true (a FC layer = a conv with kernel covering the whole input). FC layers are **linear** (not non-linear), require a **fixed input size**, and after $XW$ the feature dimension is $M$, so batch-norm parameters have length $M$, not $D$.
questions.extend: FC ⇔ convolution: an FC layer on a flattened $C\times H\times W$ input equals a conv filter of kernel size $H\times W$ over $C$ channels. The fixed-input-size limitation is exactly what fully-convolutional nets remove.

questions.q: Given a batch of images $(8\times3\times8\times8)$ and the task of detecting eyes of people, name two disadvantages of using a fully-connected network. (Why do convolutions fit segmentation/detection better than FC?)
questions.answer:
(i) FC layers extract only **global features**, while detecting eyes requires **local features** of small windows.
(ii) The resolution is too small / FC throws away spatial structure, so fine local detail is lost.

*(SS22 5.10 equivalent:)* fully-connected layers process global information from the entire image, whereas convolutions look at local neighbourhoods — better suited to localized tasks like semantic segmentation/detection.
questions.extend: FC = global + fixed size + many parameters; convolutions = local + translation-equivariant + parameter-efficient. Localized vision tasks want locality.

questions.q: Given a batch of images of shape $(8\times3\times8\times8)$, how do you process them through a fully-connected layer? What is the shape of $W$ for logistic regression with BCE loss?
questions.answer: **Flatten** each image to a vector of size $3\times8\times8 = 192$, giving $X_{8\times192}$. For logistic regression (1 output logit) the weight matrix is $W_{192\times1}$ (plus a scalar bias), and a sigmoid + BCE produces the binary prediction.
questions.extend: FC layers need flattened, fixed-size inputs: $(N, C, H, W) \to (N, C\cdot H\cdot W)$. Output neurons = classes (or 1 for binary/regression).

questions.q: A network is `Linear(100→50) → Linear(50→10)` with no activation in between. How many effective layers does it have, and how would you fix it to learn non-linear patterns?
questions.answer: Effectively **one** linear layer ($100\to10$), since composing two affine maps yields a single affine map $X(W_1W_2)+\dots$. To learn non-linear patterns, insert a **non-linear activation** (e.g. ReLU) between the two linear layers.
questions.extend: More layers ≠ more capacity unless separated by non-linearities. The activation is what breaks the linear collapse.

knowledge_points.title: Backpropagation
knowledge_points.recap: Backprop applies the chain rule from the loss backwards. For a FC layer $Y=XW+b$ with upstream gradient $\text{dout}=\frac{\partial L}{\partial Y}$: $\frac{\partial L}{\partial X}=\text{dout}\,W^\top$, $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W}=X^\top\text{dout}$, $\frac{\partial L}{\partial b}=\sum_n \text{dout}$. Because the loss is a **scalar** $L:\mathbb{R}^{N}\times\mathbb{R}^{M}\to\mathbb{R}$ we only ever need gradients of scalars, never full Jacobians.

questions.q: For a FC layer $Y = XW + b$ ($X_{N\times D}$, $W_{D\times M}$) with upstream gradient $\text{dout}=\frac{\partial L}{\partial Y}$: (a) what are the dimensions of $b$ and $\text{dout}$? (b) what is $\frac{\partial L}{\partial X}$ and its dimensions?
questions.answer:
(a) $b$ has shape $1\times M$; $\text{dout}=\frac{\partial L}{\partial Y}$ has shape $N\times M$.
(b) $\frac{\partial L}{\partial X} = \text{dout}\cdot W^\top$, of shape $N\times D$.
questions.extend: Dimension trick: the gradient w.r.t. a variable has the **same shape** as that variable. $\frac{\partial L}{\partial X}$ is $N\times D$ like $X$; $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W}=X^\top\text{dout}$ is $D\times M$ like $W$.

questions.q: For $y = XW + ZR + T + A$ (with $X,Z\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times D}$, $W,R\in\mathbb{R}^{D\times M}$, $T,A\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times M}$) and $L=\sum_{i,j}y_{ij}$, what are $\frac{\partial L}{\partial Z}$ and $\frac{\partial L}{\partial A}$?
questions.answer: $\frac{\partial L}{\partial Z} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial y}\cdot R^\top$ (matrix multiplication), and $\frac{\partial L}{\partial A} = \mathbf{1}_{N\times M}\odot\frac{\partial L}{\partial y}$ (element-wise). Since $L=\sum y_{ij}$, $\frac{\partial L}{\partial y}=\mathbf{1}_{N\times M}$.
questions.extend: Each additive term backprops independently. A matrix-multiply term ($ZR$) gives $\text{dout}\,R^\top$; an additive term ($A$) just passes the gradient through element-wise.

questions.q: Which property of loss functions lets us perform backpropagation without computing full Jacobians?
questions.answer: The loss is a **scalar function** $L:\mathbb{R}^{N}\times\mathbb{R}^{M}\to\mathbb{R}$, and all variables in a network are ultimately **scalars** assembled into matrices/tensors. We only ever need $\frac{\partial L}{\partial v}$ for scalar $v$, so gradients have the same shape as the variables — no Jacobian matrices required.
questions.extend: A vector→vector layer would need a Jacobian, but a scalar loss collapses everything to gradients (one number per parameter), making backprop cheap.

questions.q: (Toy network, Leaky ReLU $\alpha=0.5$) Backpropagation problems ask you to do a forward pass then update a weight via SGD. What is the derivative of Leaky ReLU used in the backward pass?
questions.answer: Leaky ReLU $f(x)=\max(\alpha x, x)$ has derivative $f'(x)=1$ for $x>0$ and $f'(x)=\alpha$ for $x<0$ (here $\alpha=0.5$). In backprop you multiply the upstream gradient by this local derivative element-wise, depending on the sign of each pre-activation from the forward pass.
questions.extend: Always cache forward-pass signs/positions: ReLU/Leaky-ReLU need the input sign, MaxPool needs the argmax position, to route gradients correctly.

questions.q: During backprop through $Y=XW+b$ you are given $\text{dout}$ of shape $N\times M$. Write $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W}$ and verify its shape.
questions.answer: $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W} = X^\top\,\text{dout}$. Shapes: $X^\top$ is $D\times N$, $\text{dout}$ is $N\times M$, product is $D\times M$ — the same shape as $W$. ✓
questions.extend: Use the shape-matching rule to remember whether to transpose and which side to multiply: the result must match the parameter's shape.

questions.q: In PyTorch, what is/are the correct order(s) of steps when training a model with gradient descent?
questions.answer: **Valid:** any order where the gradients are **zeroed before backpropagation** and parameters are **updated after** it — both *zero → forward → backward → update* and *forward → zero → backward → update* work. **Invalid:** backprop before zeroing, or updating before backprop.
questions.extend: Invariant: `optimizer.zero_grad()` before `loss.backward()`, and `optimizer.step()` after it. The forward pass may sit before or after the zeroing.

options.text: Zero the gradients → forward pass → backpropagation → update parameters.
Forward pass → zero the gradients → backpropagation → update parameters.
Backpropagation → zero the gradients → update parameters.
Forward pass → update parameters → backpropagation.

knowledge_points.title: Activation Functions (Sigmoid, Tanh, ReLU & variants)
knowledge_points.recap: Activations add **non-linearity**. **Sigmoid** $\sigma(x)=\frac{1}{1+e^{-x}}\in(0,1)$ — not zero-centered, saturates → vanishing gradients. **Tanh** $\in(-1,1)$ — zero-centered, still saturates. **ReLU** $\max(0,x)$ — no positive saturation, cheap, but not zero-centered and can 'die'. **Leaky/Parametric ReLU** add a slope $\alpha$ for $x<0$ to avoid dead units. **ELU** smooths the negative side.

questions.q: What is the purpose of activation functions in a neural network?
questions.answer: They introduce **non-linearity**, allowing the network to learn complex (non-linear) patterns. Without them, a stack of layers collapses to a single linear transformation regardless of depth.
questions.extend: One sentence the exam loves: *activations make the network non-linear, so depth adds expressive power.*

questions.q: Give two advantages of Tanh over Sigmoid.
questions.answer: (1) Tanh is **zero-centered** (range $(-1,1)$), while Sigmoid is not (range $(0,1)$) — zero-centered activations avoid the zig-zag gradient problem. (2) Tanh has a **larger maximum gradient** than Sigmoid, so gradients vanish a little less aggressively.
questions.extend: Both still saturate at the tails → both suffer vanishing gradients in deep nets. Zero-centering is Tanh's main edge; ReLU later beats both on the vanishing-gradient front.

questions.q: Explain the differences between ReLU, LeakyReLU and Parametric ReLU.
questions.answer: **ReLU:** outputs constant $0$ for negative inputs. **LeakyReLU:** uses a small **pre-defined** slope $\alpha$ for negative values (e.g. $0.01$). **Parametric ReLU (PReLU):** the negative slope is a **learnable** parameter (one per channel, or one shared).
questions.extend: Progression: ReLU (dead for $x<0$) → LeakyReLU (fixed leak) → PReLU (learned leak). The leak fixes the dying-ReLU problem by keeping a non-zero gradient for negatives.

questions.q: Mention 2 drawbacks of ReLU and explain how they affect optimization.
questions.answer:
(1) **Not zero-centered** → all weights of a neuron can only change with the same sign, causing a **zig-zag** path and slower training.
(2) **Dying ReLU**: neurons stuck at $0$ output have **zero gradient** → those weights never update (a form of vanishing gradient).
(Also: ReLU is **not differentiable at $0$**.)
questions.extend: Dying ReLU is fixed by Leaky/Parametric ReLU or careful (He/Kaiming) initialization. The non-zero-centered issue motivates BatchNorm and zero-centered activations.

questions.q: Explain the vanishing gradient problem and describe one method to help solve it.
questions.answer:
**Vanishing gradients:** as gradients propagate backward through many layers (especially through saturating activations like Sigmoid/Tanh), they become exponentially smaller, so early (shallow) layers update very slowly or not at all.
**One solution:** **skip connections** (residual blocks) that create a 'highway' for gradients to flow directly. (Also: ReLU activations, BatchNorm, proper initialization.)
questions.extend: Causes: saturating activations + deep stacks + bad init. Fixes: ReLU, He/Xavier init, BatchNorm, residual/skip connections.

questions.q: For LeakyReLU, if we take $\alpha < 0$, what property of the function do we lose?
questions.answer: We lose **monotonicity** — with $\alpha<0$ the negative branch slopes the wrong way, so the function is no longer monotonically increasing (and behaves erratically). (The intended behaviour of a small positive leak is to keep a non-zero, same-sign gradient for negatives.)
questions.extend: LeakyReLU wants a small **positive** $\alpha$ (e.g. 0.01–0.2). A negative slope breaks the 'pass a bit of the negative signal through' intent.

questions.q: A sigmoid layer ...

options.text: ... could be used only on the logits of a classification neural network.
... could boost performance when used in linear regression on normalized data with mean=0, std=1.
... maps all values into the interval $[-0.5, 0.5]$.
... is a zero-centered non-linear activation function.

questions.answer: **None are correct.** Sigmoid maps to $(0,1)$ (not $[-0.5,0.5]$), is **not** zero-centered, is not restricted to only the logits, and squashing a regression output through sigmoid would hurt (it bounds and distorts the output). A valid I2DL multiple-choice can have **zero** correct answers.
questions.extend: Remember: any number of options (including none) can be correct in I2DL MC. Sigmoid: range $(0,1)$, not zero-centered, saturates.

questions.q: Which of the following introduce non-linearity / are NOT suitable as activation functions?

options.text: LeakyReLU (with $\alpha\neq0$) introduces non-linearity.
Convolution introduces non-linearity.
MaxPool introduces non-linearity.
$\text{ReLU}(x) - \text{ReLU}(-x)$ is a valid non-linear activation.

questions.answer: LeakyReLU introduces non-linearity. **Convolution is linear** (so it does not). MaxPool is non-linear but is a pooling op, not an activation. $\text{ReLU}(x)-\text{ReLU}(-x) = x$ — the identity — so it is **linear** and adds no non-linearity.
questions.extend: Trap functions: $\text{ReLU}(x)-\text{ReLU}(-x)=x$ and $\log(\text{ReLU}(x)+1)$-type expressions. Check whether the composition actually bends the line.

questions.q: You build a deep net with Sigmoid activations and training stalls — early layers barely change. Name the likely cause and two concrete fixes.
questions.answer: Likely **vanishing gradients** from sigmoid saturation across many layers. Fixes: (1) replace Sigmoid with **ReLU/LeakyReLU**; (2) add **skip/residual connections** and/or **BatchNorm**, and use **He/Xavier initialization**.
questions.extend: Sigmoid's derivative peaks at $0.25$, so each layer can shrink the gradient by ≥4×; 10 layers → ≥$4^{10}$ attenuation.

questions.q: What is the 'dead ReLU' problem and how do you solve it?
questions.answer: Some ReLU units have **always-negative pre-activations**, so they output 0 for every input and their **gradient is always 0** — those weights never update ('dead'). Fix: use **Leaky ReLU** (non-zero negative slope) or **Kaiming/He initialization**.
questions.extend: Dead ReLU = permanently-zero neuron. Leaky/Parametric ReLU keep a small negative-side gradient so units can recover.

questions.q: Is $f(z)=-0.25z$ a suitable activation function? Explain.
questions.answer: **No.** It is a **linear** function, so it introduces **no non-linearity** — stacking such layers collapses to a single linear map, defeating the purpose of a deep network.
questions.extend: Any purely linear activation is useless as a non-linearity; the network would be equivalent to one affine layer.

questions.q: Is $y=\cos(x)$ appropriate as an activation function in a backprop network? Why or why not?
questions.answer: **Not a good choice.** $\cos$ is **non-monotonic and periodic**, so very different inputs map to the same output and its derivative $-\sin(x)$ oscillates in sign — making optimization unstable and ambiguous. Standard activations are monotonic.
questions.extend: Good activations are typically monotonic with well-behaved gradients; periodic functions create many equivalent inputs and sign-flipping gradients.

questions.q: Which statements about activation functions are true?
questions.answer: **True:** ReLU units can 'die' (always-negative pre-activation → zero gradient); Tanh outputs lie in $(-1,1)$. **False:** Sigmoid is **not** zero-centred; a purely **linear** activation cannot give a deep net non-linear power (the layers collapse to one).
questions.extend: ReLU → dying units; Tanh → zero-centred (-1,1); Sigmoid → not zero-centred; linear activation → no added power.

options.text: ReLU can suffer from 'dying' units with zero gradient.
Sigmoid is a zero-centred activation.
Tanh outputs lie in the interval $(-1,1)$.
A purely linear activation still lets a deep network learn non-linear boundaries.

questions.q: Which of the following is/are true about activation functions?
questions.answer: **True:** *Sigmoid can lead to vanishing gradients* (it saturates). **False:** $\log(\text{ReLU}(x+1))$ is a poor activation (undefined where $\text{ReLU}(x+1)=0$); *Parametric ReLU* avoids the Dead-ReLU problem via its learnable negative slope; *Tanh* still saturates and **does** suffer vanishing gradients despite being zero-centered.
questions.extend: Zero-centering (Tanh) helps optimization but does NOT remove saturation/vanishing gradients.

options.text: Sigmoid can lead to vanishing gradients.
$\log(\text{ReLU}(x+1))$ is suitable to add non-linearity to a network.
Parametric ReLU always has the 'Dead ReLU' problem.
Tanh will not lead to vanishing gradients because it is zero-centered.

questions.q: Describe what 'vanishing gradient' is in the context of backpropagation through activation functions (1p). Give one example of a function that suffers from it (1p).
questions.answer: When activation functions **saturate**, their derivative becomes ~0, so by the **chain rule** the gradient of the loss w.r.t. parameters gets **extremely small in the early (shallow) layers** → those layers barely learn. **Example: Sigmoid or Tanh** (ReLU does not suffer this).
questions.extend: Vanishing gradient ≠ dead ReLU: it's saturation-driven shrinkage through many layers, classically from Sigmoid/Tanh.

knowledge_points.title: Softmax & Numerical Stability
knowledge_points.recap: Softmax turns logits into a probability distribution: $\text{Softmax}(x)_i = \dfrac{e^{x_i}}{\sum_j e^{x_j}}$. It is **shift-invariant**: subtracting $\max(x)$ from every logit leaves the output unchanged but prevents overflow of $e^{x_i}$.

questions.q: Softmax can suffer numerical instability. Show that subtracting $\max(x)$ from every logit does not change the output: $\text{Softmax}(x-\max(x))_i = \text{Softmax}(x)_i$.
questions.answer: $\text{Softmax}(x-\max(x))_i = \dfrac{e^{x_i-\max(x)}}{\sum_j e^{x_j-\max(x)}} = \dfrac{e^{x_i}e^{-\max(x)}}{e^{-\max(x)}\sum_j e^{x_j}} = \dfrac{e^{x_i}}{\sum_j e^{x_j}} = \text{Softmax}(x)_i.$ The common factor $e^{-\max(x)}$ cancels.
questions.extend: Subtracting the max makes the largest exponent $e^0=1$, so no overflow — the standard 'log-sum-exp' stabilization trick, output identical.

questions.q: Why is Softmax paired with cross-entropy rather than MSE for multi-class classification?
questions.answer: Softmax outputs a probability distribution; **cross-entropy** is the natural log-likelihood loss for distributions and yields a clean, well-scaled gradient (softmax+CE gives gradient $\hat y - y$). MSE on probabilities has small, vanishing gradients when predictions saturate and does not correspond to maximum likelihood for categorical targets.
questions.extend: Softmax + cross-entropy is a matched pair: the combined backward pass simplifies to (prediction − one-hot target).

questions.q: For a 3-class problem with softmax probabilities $y_k$ and cross-entropy loss $L=-\ln(y_3)$ (true label 3), compute $\frac{\partial L}{\partial z_k}$. What would you cache in the forward pass?
questions.answer: $\frac{\partial L}{\partial z_k}=y_k-\mathbb{1}[k=3]$: i.e. $y_3-1$ for the true class and $y_k$ for the others. **Cache** the ground-truth label and the softmax outputs $y_k$ to accelerate the backward pass.
questions.extend: The clean softmax+CE gradient is $\hat y - y$ (prediction minus one-hot) — one of the most important results to memorize.

knowledge_points.title: Classification Losses (Cross-Entropy & BCE)
knowledge_points.recap: **(Multi-class) Cross-Entropy** $L=-\sum_i y_i\log\hat y_i$ with Softmax outputs. **Binary Cross-Entropy** with a single sigmoid output for 2 classes. A freshly-initialized classifier outputs near-uniform probabilities, so the expected first-iteration CE loss is $-\log(1/C)=\log C$.

questions.q: Training a network to classify a 2-class dataset — what is a good choice of (final activation, loss function)?

options.text: (TanH, Binary Cross Entropy)
(ReLU, Mean Squared Error (L2))
(Sigmoid, Binary Cross Entropy)
(Softmax, (Multi-class) Cross Entropy)

questions.answer:
Correct: **(Sigmoid, BCE)** — one logit for binary; and **(Softmax, Cross-Entropy)** — two logits as a multi-class problem. TanH/ReLU outputs aren't probabilities, and MSE is a regression loss.
*(SS22 4.4 equivalent for >2 classes:)* use **(Multi-class) Cross-Entropy.*
questions.extend: Classification heads: binary → Sigmoid + BCE (1 neuron); multi-class → Softmax + CE ($C$ neurons). Regression → linear output + MSE/MAE.

questions.q: Using cross-entropy on $C$ classes, what is the expected loss after the very first iteration (random init), and why? (e.g. $C=100$; $C=4$; binary BCE.)
questions.answer:
A randomly-initialized network outputs roughly uniform probabilities $1/C$, so expected CE $= -\log(1/C)=\log C$.
• $C=100$: $-\log(0.01)\approx 4.6$.
• $C=4$: $-\ln(0.25)\approx 1.39$.
• Binary BCE: $-\log(0.5)=\log 2\approx 0.69$ (SS21 2.2).
questions.extend: Sanity-check your training: if initial loss ≫ $\log C$, something is wrong (bad init/scaling). $\log C$ is the 'no information' baseline.

questions.q: BCE/CE: (a) what causes numerical instability and how to fix it? (b) how many output neurons does BCE use? (c) why multiply by $-1$?
questions.answer:
(a) **Instability** comes from $\log(0)$. Fix by **clipping** logits/probabilities to $[\,\epsilon, 1-\epsilon\,]$.
(b) BCE uses **1** output neuron (binary).
(c) We multiply by $-1$ because $-\log(x)\ge 0$ for $0<x\le 1$, making the loss positive and minimized as $\hat y\to$ target.
questions.extend: $-\log$ of a probability is a positive 'surprise' — small for confident-correct, large for confident-wrong. We minimize surprise.

questions.q: Your multi-class classifier shows a near-zero training loss but only ~10% accuracy on 10 balanced classes. (Hint: it uses Sigmoid instead of Softmax with CE.) What went wrong?
questions.answer: With independent **sigmoids** (instead of softmax) the network can push every class output toward 1, so a per-class CE that doesn't penalize false positives reports a tiny loss while argmax accuracy stays at chance. Use **Softmax + multi-class CE** so the outputs compete and sum to 1.
questions.extend: Mirrors SS24 5.3: CCE with sigmoid outputs ‘doesn't punish false positives’. Match the output activation to the loss.

questions.q: A softmax classifier over 20 classes is randomly initialized and trained with categorical cross-entropy. What is the approximate loss in the first epoch?
questions.answer: $L\approx-\log\!\big(\tfrac{1}{20}\big)=\log 20\approx 3.0$ — a random init outputs roughly uniform probabilities $1/C$, so the expected initial CE is $-\log(1/C)=\log C$.
questions.extend: Sanity check: initial CE should sit near $\log C$. Far above ⇒ bad init/scaling; this is the 'no information' baseline.

questions.q: Explain why minimizing the cross-entropy $H(p,q)$ between class probabilities $q$ (network) and target $p$ is equivalent to minimizing the KL divergence $D_{KL}(p\|q)=H(p,q)-H(p)$.
questions.answer: Because $H(p)$ — the **entropy of the fixed target distribution** $p$ — does **not depend on the model parameters**; it is a **constant** with zero gradient. So $\min_\theta D_{KL}(p\|q)=\min_\theta\big(H(p,q)-H(p)\big)=\min_\theta H(p,q)$.
questions.extend: Dropping a parameter-independent constant doesn't change the argmin — that's why CE training = KL minimization.

questions.q: Write down the formula for the binary cross-entropy loss (1p) and define all the variables (1p).
questions.answer: $L(\hat y_i,y_i)=-\big(y_i\log\hat y_i+(1-y_i)\log(1-\hat y_i)\big)$, or batched $L=-\frac1N\sum_{i=1}^{N}\big(y_i\log\hat y_i+(1-y_i)\log(1-\hat y_i)\big)$. Here $y_i\in\{0,1\}$ is the **ground-truth label**, $\hat y_i\in(0,1)$ is the **model's predicted probability**, and $N$ is the **batch size**.
questions.extend: BCE needs a single probability output $\hat y_i$ (sigmoid); the two log terms reward the correct class for label 1 vs 0.

knowledge_points.title: Regression Losses (MSE & MAE)
knowledge_points.recap: **MSE / L2** $\frac{1}{N}\sum(\hat y-y)^2$ — smooth, differentiable, but sensitive to outliers (squares large errors). **MAE / L1** $\frac{1}{N}\sum|\hat y-y|$ — robust to outliers, but not differentiable at 0 (handled by sub-gradient). $N$ = total number of residuals (over batch × all output dims).

questions.q: In depth estimation most pixels have very small loss but a few have very large loss. Out of [MAE, MSE, BCE, CE], which loss is better and why?
questions.answer: **MAE (L1)** is better: it is **less sensitive to outliers** than MSE (which squares the large errors), so it fits the majority of small-residual pixels better instead of being dominated by a few huge residuals.
questions.extend: MSE punishes outliers quadratically → a few bad pixels dominate. MAE weights all residuals linearly → robust. (BCE/CE are classification losses, irrelevant here.)

questions.q: (a) Using MSE on a batch of images of shape $(4\times3\times8\times8)$, what value $N$ do we divide by? (b) Why can MAE still be used despite not being differentiable at $x=0$?
questions.answer:
(a) $N = 4\times3\times8\times8 = 768$ — the **total number of residuals** (every scalar output element across the batch).
(b) We assume **local differentiability**; the single non-differentiable point $x=0$ is handled with a sub-gradient and almost never hit exactly, so optimization is fine.
questions.extend: MSE/MAE average over *all* output scalars, not just batch size. The $x=0$ kink in MAE is measure-zero and harmless in practice.

questions.q: First iteration: some logits are above 1000 while ground-truth values are in $[0,1]$. Using MSE, what optimization problem do you expect?
questions.answer: The residuals (and thus gradients) are **enormous**, so you should expect the **exploding gradient** problem — the update step is far too large and the network diverges.
questions.extend: Always normalize inputs/targets and initialize sensibly; gigantic residuals → gigantic gradients → divergence. (Mirrors the need for normalization in Ch I.)

questions.q: Compared to the L1 loss, the L2 loss... (which statements hold?)
questions.answer: L2 (MSE) **has a different optimum** than L1 and is **more sensitive to outliers** (it is *not* robust to outliers). L2 is **not** sparser than L1 (L1 promotes sparsity) and is not especially costly to compute. So among the typical options, only “has a different optimum” is true.
questions.extend: L2 optimum = mean of targets; L1 optimum = median (hence L1's robustness). L1 → sparser solutions in the regularization sense.

questions.q: You train a regression model and a few mislabeled samples with huge errors dominate training. Which loss switch helps, and what is the trade-off?
questions.answer: Switch from **MSE to MAE (L1)** — it down-weights the influence of large-error outliers (its optimum is the median). Trade-off: MAE has a constant-magnitude gradient and a non-smooth point at 0, so convergence near the optimum can be slower/less smooth than MSE. (Huber loss blends both.)
questions.extend: Outlier-heavy → L1 / Huber. Clean, Gaussian-noise data → L2. The choice encodes your assumption about the noise distribution.

questions.q: For a regression task with labels in $[-1,1]$, which final activations $f(x)$ could be used?
questions.answer: **Valid (bounded to $[-1,1]$):** $2\sigma(x)-1$, $\tanh(10x)$, and $\min(\max(-1,x),1)$ (clamp). **Invalid:** $\max(-1,x)$ is unbounded above, so it can exceed 1.
questions.extend: Match the output range to the label range: sigmoid/tanh/clamp bound both sides; a one-sided ReLU-like clamp does not.

options.text: $f(x) = 2\sigma(x) - 1$ (sigmoid).
$f(x) = \tanh(10x)$
$f(x) = \min(\max(-1, x), 1)$ (clamp)

knowledge_points.title: Linear & Logistic Regression
knowledge_points.recap: **Linear regression** has a closed-form solution $W=(X^\top X)^{-1}X^\top Y$, but it is infeasible at scale (forming/inverting a huge matrix) → use iterative gradient-based training. **Logistic regression** = linear layer + sigmoid + cross-entropy: a linear, supervised, binary classifier.

questions.q: (a) Fitting 5 billion points each with 100 features — is it feasible to find the optimum without a neural network? (b) How do we turn a linear (affine-only) model into a classifier?
questions.answer:
(a) **No.** Linear regression has a closed-form solution $W=(X^\top X)^{-1}X^\top Y$, but it requires loading a $5\!\times\!10^9 \times 100$ matrix into RAM and inverting it — infeasible. Use **iterative, batched gradient descent** through a network instead.
(b) Add a **sigmoid** at the end and use a **cross-entropy** loss → logistic regression / classifier.
questions.extend: Closed-form OLS is $O(\text{features}^3)$ to invert and needs the whole dataset in memory — fine for small problems, hopeless at deep-learning scale. Sigmoid + CE = the bridge from regression to classification.

questions.q: Why might a closed-form linear solution be 'superior' for a linearly-distributed dataset?
questions.answer: Because it has a **closed-form solution**: $W=(X^\top X)^{-1}X^\top Y$ computes the exact optimum directly (no iteration, no learning rate, no convergence issues) — mathematically superior when the data truly is linear and small enough to invert.
questions.extend: Closed form wins on small linear problems; iterative GD wins when data is huge or the problem is non-linear (i.e. real deep learning).

questions.q: Logistic regression (check all that apply).

options.text: Is a linear function.
Is a supervised learning algorithm.
Uses a type of cross-entropy loss.
Allows to perform binary classification.

questions.answer: **All four** are correct. Despite the name, logistic regression is a **linear, supervised, binary classifier** trained with (binary) cross-entropy.
questions.extend: ‘Regression’ in the name refers to the linear score; the sigmoid + CE turn it into a classifier. Decision boundary is linear.

questions.q: A friend claims 'logistic regression can only draw a straight boundary, so it's useless for the XOR problem.' Is the premise right, and how do you fix the model?
questions.answer: The premise is **correct** — logistic regression is linear and cannot separate XOR with a single straight boundary. Fix: add **non-linear features** or **hidden layers + activations** (an MLP) so the model can learn a non-linear decision boundary.
questions.extend: Linear model ⇒ linear boundary. Depth + non-linear activations are what break that limit.
